transcript.text: This video is what I think about biology. I was inspired to do this one because I saw a very interesting series of videos by a biologist called Bruce Lipton.
Bruce Lipton. I recommend seeing his videos. There's one that's 17 10-minute segment videos long. He's very animated and excited because he's trying to explain to us the new biologist.
The new way in which we should look at biology. This is an old guy so he knows how we thought about biology from before and what we think about biology now. He's very emotional about it.
Basically, what we thought about biology before is supposedly everything that happens in our body comes from our nucleus of our cells, where all the DNA is.
Back in World War II and before, people were really into eugenics, having to sterilize certain people just because they don't perform so well in memorization tests or whatever.
And then, you know, racism, justifying racism, you know, thinking that everything's written in your genes and there's nothing really you're going to be able to do about it.
So, you know, this group of people over here are stupid, need to be wiped out the face of the planet, like this race or this other people here.
And Bruce Lipton is saying, no, no, no, no, no, no. We are not subject to our genes.
Subject to our DNA. Because DNA does not work like that. All DNA is, is a series of possibilities.
If you're born into a family, say, of basketball players, you have more of a possibility of a chance to be a basketball player.
If you're born in a family of mathematicians, you have more of a chance, a possibility to be a mathematician because you have more of those genes.

But that compared to somebody born into a family of mathematicians, like if comparing two people, somebody adopted who, their biological family is a bunch of mathematicians and they're adopted by a bunch of farmers.
This person is going to grow up to be a farmer, more likely to grow up to be a farmer, than the person whose biological family are farmers and then they grow up in a family of mathematicians.
The effects are very, are much more prone to your environment than to your genetics because your genes are, are not out there, you know, controlling everything.
They are not out there.
They are, they are wrapped up in a sheath that can only be unlocked by a certain protein in a certain configuration.
That protein can only be, uh, signaled by a protein, by another protein on the outside of the cell in a certain configuration.

So, um, you know, somebody could have cancer gene in their body, their whole life, and that gene will never have, will never mean anything to them because it's behind the sheath latent their whole life.
